title: Sets (The Unique Club) 🎫
description: Store items super fast, but no duplicates allowed!

slides.title: No Duplicates Allowed! 🚫
slides.content:
# What is a Set?

A **Set** is the final of the three main Swift collections.

Like an Array, it stores a list of items of the same type. However, a Set has two massive differences:
1. **No Duplicates**: A Set guarantees that every item inside it is 100% unique.
2. **Unordered**: A Set does NOT track what order items were added in.

slides.title: Building a Set 🎫
slides.content:
# Explicit Declaration required

Because an Array and a Set look identical when creating them with square brackets, you MUST explicitly tell Swift you wanted a Set using Type Annotation.

```swift
var lottoNumbers: Set<Int> = [10, 25, 40, 10]
print(lottoNumbers)
```
If you print this, you will see `[40, 10, 25]`. It destroyed the duplicate `10`, and it scrambled the order!

quizData.question: What happens if you run: `let safeWords: Set<String> = ["Apple", "Apple"]`?

options.text: The Set will contain two Apples.
The Set will contain exactly one Apple.

quizData.explanation: Sets automatically and silently filter out all duplicate values upon creation or insertion.

slides.title: Why Use a Set? ⚡️
slides.content:
# Blazing Fast Lookups

If Sets jumble your data and delete duplicates, why use them instead of an Array?

Because Sets are **incredibly fast**.

Asking an Array `hasItem("Apple")` requires checking every single item 1 by 1. Asking a Set `contains("Apple")` happens instantly, even if there are ten million items in the Set! It's math magic!

slides.title: See It in Action
slides.content: Notice how `.insert` adds an item, but the duplicate is ignored!
slides.codeSnippet:
var attendees: Set<String> = ["Alice", "Bob"]

attendees.insert("Charlie")
// Bob tries to enter twice
attendees.insert("Bob")

print(attendees)
print("Is Alice here? \(attendees.contains("Alice"))")
